tasks.chapterTitle: Part Two: You are the President and CEO of You

messages.content:
### Summary: *Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom* (Priester)

*Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom* (FAS: WoW) serves as a guide for students to transition from being a "college student" to a "successful college student." Moving away from generic "how-to" facts in favor of authentic, reflective storytelling, the text provides insights from SUNY students, alumni, and educators to help readers navigate the realities of higher education.

#### **Key Points**

*   **Success is Non-Linear:** The author emphasizes that academic success is not a straight path but a series of hurdles and adaptations [S.17]. Students are encouraged to define success based on their own personal and professional goals rather than following a prescribed formula [S.7, S.17, S.18].
*   **The Power of Personal Agency:** Students are the "President and CEO" of their own education [S.5]. Taking responsibility for one's choices is critical; this includes being accountable for grades, seeking help early, and utilizing campus resources like writing centers, career services, and office hours [S.18, S.24, S.25].
*   **Engagement and Connectivity:** Success often stems from feeling connected to the campus community. Engaging in extracurriculars and building relationships with staff and faculty can provide a necessary support system that improves both the college experience and future career prospects [S.23, S.25].
*   **Learning Through Failure:** Real-life stories—such as failing an online course or choosing the wrong major—illustrate that setbacks are valuable learning opportunities. They demonstrate that students must identify their preferred learning styles and hold themselves accountable to remain on track [S.24, S.25].
*   **Navigating Life Beyond the Classroom:** College is an opportunity to cultivate self-awareness, explore cultural diversity, and refine critical thinking skills. It is as much about personal growth, understanding one's values, and broadening one's worldview as it is about earning a degree [S.18, S.27].

#### **Core Practical Guidance**
The author provides several "Rules of Thumb" for incoming students to ensure they start on a strong foundation [S.18]:
*   **Time Management:** Treat the academic load with the rigor of a full-time job.
*   **Professionalism:** Master basic etiquette (e.g., proper email formatting, putting away phones in class).
*   **Proactivity:** "Early is on time, on time is late, and late is unacceptable" [S.18].
*   **Institutional Awareness:** Understand policies like FERPA and always utilize faculty and campus services before reaching a point of crisis [S.18, S.24, S.25].

Lesson 5: Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom (Priester) - Presentation5 slides
Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom
  • A comprehensive guide for thriving in college.
  • Features authentic, self-authored stories from SUNY students.
  • Provides practical techniques to manage academic demands.

### Summary of Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom

*Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom (FAS: WoW)*, authored by Dr. Thomas C. Priester, is an open-access textbook designed to help college students navigate the transition to higher education and thrive academically [S.3, S.7, S.18].

**Core Philosophy and Approach**
The text distinguishes between being a "college student" and a "successful college student" by emphasizing self-awareness and personal growth [S.7, S.18]. Unlike traditional textbooks that focus heavily on static facts or generic "how-to" guides, this book is primarily composed of true-to-life stories, essays, and advice from SUNY students, alumni, and faculty [S.11, S.17, S.18]. This approach reflects the author's discovery that students connect more deeply with personal narratives than with abstract data [S.17].

**Structure and Content**
The book is organized into three thematic parts:
- Part One: Your Solid Foundation (The basics of the student experience, study practices, and social support) [S.5].
- Part Two: You are the President and CEO of You (Self-management, critical thinking, time management, and passion for studies) [S.5].
- Part Three: The Future You (Career development, lifelong learning, and transitioning beyond college) [S.5].

**Educational Objectives**
The text encourages students to reflect on guiding questions that bridge the gap between their current standing and their academic and personal goals [S.18]. Key focus areas include:
- Demonstrating college readiness through effective resource utilization [S.18].
- Applying critical and creative thinking to problem-solving [S.18].
- Developing self-awareness and understanding the implications of life choices [S.18].
- Navigating cultural diversity and information management [S.18].

**Academic Success Tips**
Dr. Priester emphasizes practical discipline, advocating for habits such as arriving early, engaging with course materials, professional communication with professors, and actively utilizing campus support services [S.18]. He asserts that while experts can provide guidance, success is a unique, personal journey that students must ultimately define and pursue for themselves [S.17, S.18].

The provided text, spanning [S.23] to [S.29], emphasizes that the college experience is defined by the proactive choices students make to engage with campus resources and personal development opportunities.

Strategic Engagement and Resources
Success in college requires shifting away from stereotypical portrayals of student life toward purposeful involvement. Students are encouraged to utilize academic support services—such as tutoring, writing centers, and office hours—to enhance their learning and performance. Building a professional relationship with professors early in the semester is essential, as waiting until the end of a term to seek help is ineffective. Furthermore, staying organized through personal methods is critical for balancing academic requirements with extracurricular commitments.

Personal Responsibility and Accountability
A central theme is the internal locus of control. Students are responsible for their own academic outcomes; poor habits, such as neglecting online coursework or failing to seek assistance, lead to personal consequences like failing grades. Navigating the transition to college also involves understanding institutional policies, such as the Federal Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA), which shifts educational privacy responsibilities from parents to the student.

Career and Personal Growth
Students should view college as a holistic preparation for life, not solely for job placement. Key recommendations for a well-rounded experience include:

1. Exploring Beyond Comfort Zones: Engaging in study abroad programs and taking courses that challenge personal worldviews helps build a more nuanced understanding of global diversity.
2. Building Professional Readiness: Seeking internships and participating in clubs or organizations allows students to develop “soft skills” like leadership, communication, and teamwork, which are highly valued by employers.
3. Embracing Research: Developing critical research skills is applicable across all disciplines, fostering a habit of questioning information to make informed decisions as both a student and a citizen.
4. Active Reflection: Students should continuously assess whether their current academic path and activities align with their long-term goals, remaining open to changing majors or career directions as they gain new insights.

In summary, the transition from high school to college requires students to be mature, independent learners who take full advantage of the resources provided to them. By treating college as a space to practice the skills needed for their future, students can maximize their growth and minimize future regrets.

This document highlights several core pillars for academic and personal success. Each author contributes a specific strategy for navigating the challenges of higher education:

**Listening to Yourself ([S.37]-[S.38])**
In a world saturated with external noise and unsolicited advice, it is vital to practice "listening to yourself." By retreating into a quiet mental space and asking oneself probing, reflective questions, an individual can clarify their true desires and values. While one should always listen to and consider the perspectives of others, personal growth requires the ability to distinguish your own voice from outside opinions to make authentic life decisions.

**Mindset and Failure ([S.39]-[S.40])**
Success is often hindered by a "fixed mindset"—the belief that intelligence and abilities are static—which creates a paralyzing fear of failure. Instead, students should adopt a "growth mindset," which posits that qualities can be cultivated through effort. Failure, rather than being an unacceptable outcome, should be viewed as an opportunity for learning and progress. Embracing challenges and focusing on effort rather than immediate results are essential for personal development.

**Critical and Creative Thinking ([S.41]-[S.42])**
Critical thinking serves as an analytical tool to evaluate information, distinguish fact from fiction, and assess the reliability of sources. Creative thinking, conversely, acts as the catalyst for progress by generating inventive solutions to complex problems. Integrating both skills is critical to academic success. Collaborative group work is a particularly effective mechanism for developing these skills, as it forces students to reconcile their own analyses with the perspectives of others.

**Time Management ([S.43])**
Time management is inherently a personal endeavor; there is no "one-size-fits-all" method for productivity. Rather than adhering to a rigid, conventional process, students must identify their individual priorities and learn to manage both their time and their energy effectively. Recognizing the phenomenon of "time famine"—the feeling of being overwhelmed by tasks—is the first step toward reclaiming control over one's schedule and working in a manner that aligns with one's personal strengths.

This section provides guidance on navigating academic and career challenges through personal anecdotes and practical strategies.

**Personal Journeys and Growth**
The text highlights that overcoming obstacles, such as anxiety or the temptation to settle for "easy" paths, is essential for professional and personal fulfillment. Amie Bernstein emphasizes that campus involvement—such as joining honor societies—transformed her college experience from a struggle into a leadership opportunity (p. 43-44). Jacqueline Tiermini notes that while friends may pursue immediate financial comfort, investing in a degree often leads to greater long-term career satisfaction and stability (p. 45). Vicki L. Brown illustrates the concept of transferable skills, explaining that knowledge gained in one field (e.g., teaching) is highly applicable to diverse professional roles, such as administration or coaching (p. 46).

**Strategies for Professional Development**
Jackie Vetrano compares the job search process to online dating, offering a strategic framework for success:

1. The Résumé as a Dating Profile: A résumé serves as a first impression. It must be polished, tailored to the specific "audience" (the employer), and descriptive enough to highlight why the candidate is a strong match (p. 47).
2. Establishing Filters: Just as one filters potential partners by location or values, job seekers must establish clear filters—such as salary expectations, organizational culture, and job responsibilities—to focus their search effectively (p. 48).
3. Analyzing Postings: Evaluating job descriptions requires matching one's own skill set against the employer's stated requirements. Using diverse search techniques and setting up automated alerts can help ensure no opportunities are missed (p. 48).

Ultimately, the authors suggest that treating professional development as an active, intentional process allows individuals to move beyond a "run-of-the-mill" existence and pursue careers they truly love (p. 44).

This section serves as the concluding chapter for *Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom (FAS: WoW)*. Its key points are summarized below:

Core Objectives
The textbook aims to guide students in demonstrating competence across several essential areas, including effective study habits, technological proficiency, critical thinking, self-awareness, personal wellness, and cultural diversity.

Call to Action
Readers are encouraged to compose their own "Words of Wisdom" stories based on their personal academic experiences. These narratives should help future students bridge the gap between being a typical student and a successful one. Selected submissions may be featured in future editions of the book by emailing opensunyfas@gmail.com.

Educational Philosophy
The text emphasizes the importance of open access resources to reduce the financial burden of higher education. Unlike many other textbooks that can cost over $100, the *FAS: WoW* series offers a high-utility, student-centered, and accessible alternative for learners.
Here is a quiz for this lesson.

questions.question: Why did Dr. Priester decide to compile and publish 'Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom'?
options: He wanted to replace all existing textbooks with his own research-based theories.
He could not find a single textbook that combined research with real-life student stories.
He was required by his university to publish a book to retain his faculty position.
He wanted to generate revenue by selling the textbook to various colleges.
questions.explanation: Dr. Priester explains that he hunted for an effective and affordable textbook for his course but could not find everything he needed in one place, so he decided to write his own.

questions.question: What led Dr. Priester to change the focus of his textbook from facts and figures to personal stories?
options: He realized that facts and figures change too rapidly to keep updated.
He found that student feedback showed more engagement with and retention of real-life stories.
He wanted to lower the cost of the book by removing the research sections.
He decided that academic advice should be based entirely on opinion rather than research.
questions.explanation: After test-driving a draft with students, Priester noted that they barely glanced at the research-based data but provided very positive feedback on the personal, true-to-life stories.

questions.question: In her essay, what did 'the party' represent to Kristen Mruk?
options: Attending social events and drinking with fellow classmates.
The stereotypical college experience depicted in movies.
Engaging in campus activities and getting involved in the student community.
A way to escape academic pressure through extracurricular downtime.
questions.explanation: Mruk uses the term 'the party' as a metaphor for becoming connected to campus through involvement, jobs, and student activities, countering the negative stereotype of college social life.

questions.question: According to the preface, what is the main reason a student should document their strengths and weaknesses?
options: To provide the professor with a self-assessment for grading purposes.
To compare their personal performance against university-wide averages.
To actively develop a plan that transforms their specific weaknesses into strengths.
To ensure they have enough extracurricular activities on their transcript.
questions.explanation: The text emphasizes that reflecting on strengths and weaknesses is a foundational step in creating a plan to successfully transition from being just a 'college student' to being a 'successful college student.'

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the philosophy of 'Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom'?
options: It provides a universal, standardized roadmap to guarantee academic success.
It emphasizes that success looks different for everyone and is a journey of trial and error.
It argues that traditional academic theory is useless and should be ignored.
It prioritizes time management techniques over all other aspects of student life.
questions.explanation: The text notes that success isn't a straight line and that no single textbook can dictate how one should succeed, as success is defined differently by every individual.

cards.front: What is the primary focus of the textbook 'Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom'?
cards.back: It aims to prepare students for college success by sharing true-to-life short stories from SUNY students, employees, and alumni to provide relevant and reflective advice.

cards.front: What key distinction does the book make regarding college attendance?
cards.back: There is an important difference between being a college student and being a *successful* college student.

cards.front: Why did the author, Dr. Thomas Priester, decide to shift the focus of the textbook from 'facts and figures' to personal stories?
cards.back: Feedback from his students indicated they learned more from and connected better with 'true-to-life' stories than with academic facts and figures, which can often be found online.

cards.front: What is one general rule for academic success cited by the author regarding study time?
cards.back: For every hour in class, successful college-level work requires about two hours of out-of-class work.

cards.front: What is the author's advice regarding the importance of deadlines?
cards.back: Early is on time, on time is late, and late is unacceptable.

cards.front: According to the preface, how should students view their own academic path?
cards.back: Success is not a straight line, but often a 'hot mess of twists and turns, curves, and bumps' that requires adaptability.

cards.front: What is a key piece of professional advice provided by the author for student communication?
cards.back: Emails should always include a proper salutation, a body, and a close, and should not use texting abbreviations.

cards.front: What was the 'party' that Kristen Mruk eventually discovered on campus?
cards.back: Instead of the stereotypical party atmosphere, she discovered the 'party' meant engaging with campus involvement opportunities, such as working at the Student Union information desk.

cards.front: What is the primary purpose of the 'guiding questions' provided in the book?
cards.back: To help students reflect on their academic and personal goals and connect the dots between being a student and being a *successful* student.

cards.front: What is the author's advice regarding academic planning?
cards.back: Never academically advise yourself; seek help from official campus academic advisors.

slides.title: Foundations of Academic Success: Words of Wisdom
bullets: A comprehensive guide for thriving in college.
Features authentic, self-authored stories from SUNY students.
Provides practical techniques to manage academic demands.
slides.notes: This book uses real-life narratives to help students transition from simply attending college to becoming successful, engaged learners.

slides.title: Part One: Your Solid Foundation
bullets: Understand the true college student experience.
Prioritize campus involvement and support services (p. 23).
Develop reliable organizational systems for success.
Acknowledge personal accountability for academic outcomes (p. 24).
slides.notes: Building a foundation requires moving beyond stereotypes, utilizing campus resources, and taking full responsibility for your own educational journey.

slides.title: Part Two: You are the President and CEO of You
bullets: Practice listening to your own inner voice (p. 37).
Approach challenges as growth opportunities, not fixed failures (p. 39).
Critically evaluate advice while staying true to yourself.
Cultivate confidence to navigate your specific life path.
slides.notes: Taking ownership of your life means tuning out external noise, defining your own goals, and maintaining a growth mindset even when you fail.

slides.title: Part Three: The Future You
bullets: Transform personal struggles into future-focused motivation.
Engage actively in campus life to build relationships.
Step outside your comfort zone to develop leadership.
Define success beyond just getting by—aim for passion.
slides.notes: Your future is shaped by active engagement and the courage to persist, ensuring you take ownership of your personal and professional development.

slides.title: Conclusion: Paying It Forward
bullets: Reflect on guiding questions to identify your growth.
Share your own 'Words of Wisdom' with future students.
Embrace open access resources to support affordability.
Continue bridging the gap between student and successful student.
slides.notes: Reflecting on your journey helps solidify your learning and allows you to mentor others, keeping the cycle of success moving forward.
